{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645","id":1171355103,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5F0XXf","number":44645,"title":"Unexplained slowness in `@everywhere` remotecalls with imports","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":9159719,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MTU5NzE5","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parallel","name":"parallel","color":"f7e102","default":false,"description":"Parallel or distributed computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-03-16T17:52:22Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T15:12:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello there!

So I spent some time debugging an issue similar to https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42156 and perhaps https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/39291 and arrived at 2 different possible sources of slowness:

- `@everywhere` uses `@sync_add` from `remotecall_eval` to add Futures into a queue that gets wait()ed in the end. Unfortunately, waiting for a future seems to cause some additional processing on each remote worker, which is run serially. I managed to get fully parallel and fast imports by doing this manually (code below).
- For reasonable reasons, `@everywhere` loads all imports locally first. Which sounds like a good idea, but for unidentified reason (possibly this one) this causes a behavior where it "goes slowly through all workers" for me again.

To test it, I'm starting with:
```julia
using Distributed
addprocs(32)
```

If the program continues with
```julia
@time @everywhere workers() using Symbolics
```
I usually get something like 45 seconds. (tested on 16c dell xps, lots of RAM, the thing is not specific to Symbolics) The main problem is the way this uses CPU: First, Symbolics are imported locally (several seconds 1CPU), then there's the parallel part (~15s of all CPUs working) with actual loading, then there's the part when the results are being wait()ed for serially (another 15s, only 1 CPU works at a time), and finally there's a bit of extra parallel processing (roughly 10 seconds, for which I don't have any explanation). 

This code manages to import the package in around 17 seconds, and the CPU use profile looks correctly "full" in this case, no gaps!
```julia
@time asyncmap(fetch, [remotecall(()->Base.eval(Main, :(using Symbolics)), pid) for pid in workers()])
```
This doesn't import Symbolics locally, which should take like 3 seconds (see below), but it's still not a fair comparison.

So I tried removing the line with auto-imports from `@everywhere` at https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/4627729651a08cd15b556995f0672007884cf873/stdlib/Distributed/src/macros.jl#L200, to make the "actions" comparable. That shrunk the time from around 45 to 33 seconds. Looking at the difference, I really wanted to understand what's so bad on this (it should be 3s not 12s!), so tried adding the import using the `asyncmap` code.

Unfortunately, importing the package first throws the time back to ~45 seconds
```julia
@time begin
    using Symbolics
    asyncmap(fetch, [remotecall(()->Base.eval(Main, :(using Symbolics)), pid) for pid in workers()])
end
```
...despite the time for importing Symbolics and time for running the asyncmap should add to under 20 seconds:
```
julia> @time @eval using Symbolics
  3.233303 seconds (9.33 M allocations: 634.171 MiB, 1.71% gc time, 21.58% compilation time)
```

Also happens on other packages (e.g. JuMP), but for Symbolics it behaves particularly weird.

Questions:
- is there any explanation of what's happening?
- If there's an explanation for why the import slows the thing down, would a patch to `@everywhere` be acceptable that replaces the `@sync_add` with asynchronous waiting for the futures?
- EDIT: extra thought since yesterday: Is there any good way to debug/profile/log what's actually happening and getting processed at the workers? Obviously, throwing a `@profile` into the command doesn't reveal much.

Notably, fixing this would allow happy julia parallel computing on like 1000s of worker nodes with just basic Distributed and no dirty tricks. I managed to test the `asyncmap` on HPC with 1024 workers, loading our packages there shrinks from ~350 seconds with `@everywhere` to nice 10 seconds.

Thanks!
-mk","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1072506537","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645#issuecomment-1072506537","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44645","id":1072506537,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_7Sap","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-18T15:12:34Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T15:12:34Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"OK turns out that importing stuff _after_ the workers are added is actually causes some communication with workers, which is visibly unoptimized (and in turn slow)...

{"action":"opened","number":44671,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","id":883616820,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM40quw0","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671","number":44671,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"avoid using `@sync_add` on remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially. Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-03-18T16:42:50Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T17:33:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially. Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are high.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1072635426","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671#issuecomment-1072635426","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671","id":1072635426,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_7x4i","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-18T17:33:02Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T17:33:02Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Side thought: The use of `@sync_add` in Distributed was actually the only use in whole Julia. Since it's also not exported from `Base` nor officially documented, it might be feasible to slash it.
```
 ~/work/julia $ grep sync_add * -r
base/task.jl:macro sync_add(expr)
 ~/work/julia $ 
```

computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-03-18T16:42:50Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T23:27:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially. Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are high.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":1,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1072885365","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671#issuecomment-1072885365","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671","id":1072885365,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4_8u51","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-18T23:27:04Z","updated_at":"2022-03-18T23:27:04Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> can you validate that this actually alleviates #39291 (and show timing results here)?

The script:
```julia
using Distributed, ClusterManagers
n_workers = parse(Int, ENV["SLURM_NTASKS"])
@info "starting" n_workers
t = @timed addprocs_slurm(n_workers, topology = :master_worker)
@info "finished!" t.time
t = @timed @everywhere 1+1     #alternatively: @timed @everywhere using JuMP
@info "1+1d" t.time
```
Slurm batch:
```sh
#!/bin/sh
#SBATCH -t 20
#SBATCH -c 1
#SBATCH -n 256   # or 1024
#SBATCH --mem-per-cpu 3G

JULIA=$HOME/work/julia-git/julia

time $JULIA testfile.jl
```

Hardware: `cpu` nodes on Iris cluster of Uni.Lu HPC, see https://hpc-docs.uni.lu/systems/iris/

Results:

| version | test | 256w `@info` | 256w `time` wallclock | 1024w `@info` | 1024w `time` wallclock |
| --- | --- | ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 1.7.2 | 1+1 | 2m14s | 2m38s | **9m43s** | 10m28s |
| fda2c82 | 1+1 | 0.58s | 21s | **0.66s** | 50s |
| 1.7.2 | using JuMP | 3m26s | 4m8s | 11m1s | 11m44s |
| fda2c82 | using JuMP | 11s | 33s | 13s | 1m13s |

I'd say this pretty much confirms the hypothesis.


I didn't measure #42165, but from what I've seen from testing locally, this roughly describes the differences:
- 1.7.2 takes roughly `O(local_work + n_workers*(latency + long_precompile_time))`
- #42165 takes just `O(local_work + n_workers * small_roundtrip_latency)` (*waaaaay* faster but still not "scalable" per se)
- this takes `O(local_work + n_workers * negligible_packet_send_time + long_precompile_time)`  (the Distributed precompilation is present, but done in parallel way so it doesn't hurt at all. Also this is the most scalable we can get without reaching to complicated task spawning & result collection schemes)

I think `remotecall` is supposed to be cheap, but makes sense we may need to launch a Task to handle the cases when it is not cheap.

Yeah `remotecall` itself is super-fast (I'd say it wouldn't even need to be asynchronous, except for dodging an extra tiny piece of network latency); the "expensive" action that we're dodging here is what gets triggered on the remote from `@sync` after you use `@sync_add` on the `Future` returned from the `remotecall`.

seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/831401513","pull_request_review_id":916160138,"id":831401513,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4xji4p","diff_hunk":"@@ -479,14 +485,43 @@ macro async(expr)
         let $(letargs...)
             local task = Task($thunk)
             if $(Expr(:islocal, var))
-                put!($var, task)
+                put!($var, $(wrap(:task)))
             end
             schedule(task)
             task
         end
     end
 end
 
+# task wrapper that doesn't create exceptions wrapped in TaskFailedException
+struct UnwrapTaskFailedException
+    task::Task
+end
+
+# common code for wait&fetch for UnwrapTaskFailedException
+function unwrap_task_failed(f::Function, t::UnwrapTaskFailedException)
+    try
+        f(t.task)
+    catch ex
+        if ex isa TaskFailedException
+            throw(ex.task.exception)
+        else
+            rethrow()
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+# the unwrapping for above task wrapper (gets triggered in sync_end())
+wait(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(wait, t)
+
+# same for fetching the tasks, for convenience
+fetch(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(fetch, t)
+
+# macro for running async code that doesn't throw wrapped exceptions
+macro async_nowrap(expr)","path":"base/task.jl","position":51,"original_position":51,"commit_id":"633c4190cea62fef6bc2007558ed2e8c49c3e803","original_commit_id":"633c4190cea62fef6bc2007558ed2e8c49c3e803","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"yes that's actually better name given the thing actually _unwraps_ the exception...

remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/831920970","pull_request_review_id":916869469,"id":831920970,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM4xlhtK","diff_hunk":"@@ -479,14 +485,43 @@ macro async(expr)
         let $(letargs...)
             local task = Task($thunk)
             if $(Expr(:islocal, var))
-                put!($var, task)
+                put!($var, $(wrap(:task)))
             end
             schedule(task)
             task
         end
     end
 end
 
+# task wrapper that doesn't create exceptions wrapped in TaskFailedException
+struct UnwrapTaskFailedException
+    task::Task
+end
+
+# common code for wait&fetch for UnwrapTaskFailedException
+function unwrap_task_failed(f::Function, t::UnwrapTaskFailedException)
+    try
+        f(t.task)
+    catch ex
+        if ex isa TaskFailedException
+            throw(ex.task.exception)
+        else
+            rethrow()
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+# the unwrapping for above task wrapper (gets triggered in sync_end())
+wait(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(wait, t)
+
+# same for fetching the tasks, for convenience
+fetch(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(fetch, t)
+
+# macro for running async code that doesn't throw wrapped exceptions
+macro async_nowrap(expr)","path":"base/task.jl","position":null,"original_position":51,"commit_id":"7cda32c7a716b9e4bb42520caad8bfd3a05eb840","original_commit_id":"633c4190cea62fef6bc2007558ed2e8c49c3e803","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"ok, done.

remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are -                put!($var, task)
+                put!($var, $(wrap(:task)))
             end
             schedule(task)
             task
         end
     end

+# task wrapper that doesn't create exceptions wrapped in TaskFailedException
+struct UnwrapTaskFailedException
+    task::Task
+end
+
+# common code for wait&fetch for UnwrapTaskFailedException
+function unwrap_task_failed(f::Function, t::UnwrapTaskFailedException)
+    try
+        f(t.task)
+    catch ex
+        if ex isa TaskFailedException
+            throw(ex.task.exception)
+        else
+            rethrow()
+        end
+    end
+end
+
+# the unwrapping for above task wrapper (gets triggered in sync_end())
+wait(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(wait, t)
+
+# same for fetching the tasks, for convenience
+fetch(t::UnwrapTaskFailedException) = unwrap_task_failed(fetch, t)
+
+# macro for running async code that doesn't throw wrapped exceptions
+macro remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are remotecalls","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are computation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2022-03-18T16:42:50Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T08:42:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are high.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":2,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1076093372","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671#issuecomment-1076093372","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671","id":1076093372,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5AI-G8","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-23T08:42:14Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T08:42:14Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@tkf yes, it seems that the second "dodging" in my explanation wasn't the best word choice. :sweat_smile: 

Just to make it perfectly clear,
- the actions performed with and without this PR are the same
- the main difference is that calls for `wait()` here are forcibly interleaved (by `@async`), which is not the case of naive `wait()`s in `sync_end()`
- the main source of slowness here was that the first `wait()` caused compilation on remote side (>0.2s in my case). In consequence, calling these serially really hurt. (If `Distributed` got precompiled (#42156), the main source of slowness would mostly disappear, but the `wait()s` would still be serial (in my case ~0.05s per call) thus still a scalability problem.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1076093372/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

computation"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2022-03-18T16:42:50Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T10:44:01Z","closed_at":"2022-03-23T10:14:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/44671","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671.patch","merged_at":"2022-03-23T10:14:15Z"},"body":"It seems like `@sync_add` adds the `Futures` to a queue (`Channel`) for `@sync`, which in turn calls `wait()` for all the futures synchronously and serially (here: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/v1.7.2/base/task.jl#L358). Not only that is slightly detrimental for network operations (latencies add up), but in case of Distributed the call to wait() may actually cause some compilation on remote processes, which is also wait()ed for. In result, some operations took a great amount of "serial" processing time if executed on many workers at once.

For me, this closes #44645.

The major change can be illustrated as follows:

First add some workers:

```
using Distributed
addprocs(10)
```

and then trigger something that, for example, causes package imports on the workers:

```
using SomeTinyPackage
```

In my case (importing UnicodePlots on 10 workers), this improves the loading time over 10 workers from ~11s to ~5.5s.

This is a far bigger issue when worker count gets high. The time of the processing on each worker is usually around 0.3s, so triggering this problem even on a relatively small cluster (64 workers) causes a really annoying delay, and running `@everywhere` for the first time on reasonable clusters (I tested with 1024 workers, see #44645) usually takes more than 5 minutes. Which sucks.

Anyway, on 64 workers this reduces the "first import" time from ~30s to ~6s, and on 1024 workers this seems to reduce the time from over 5 minutes (I didn't bother to measure that precisely now, sorry) to ~11s.

Related issues:
- Probably fixes #39291.
- #42156 is a kinda complementary -- it removes the most painful source of   slowness (the 0.3s precompilation on the workers), but the fact that the wait()ing is serial remains a problem if the network latencies are high.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":2,"confused":0,"heart":3,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1076218387","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/44671#issuecomment-1076218387","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44671","id":1076218387,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5AJcoT","user":{"login":"exaexa","id":271543,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI3MTU0Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/271543?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa","html_url":"https://github.com/exaexa","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/exaexa/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-23T10:44:01Z","updated_at":"2022-03-23T10:44:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"PS @KristofferC is there any chance for backporting this to 1.6 or 1.7 branches?
